coredata.dc:title: Scalable preparation of high-dispersion g-C3N4 via GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation for accelerating cement hydration
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Xu, Peng
Yu, Yang
Xing, Feng
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Tremendous progress has been made in the employment of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials in cement composites. However, high cost and poor dispersion of 2D nanomaterials immensely hinder their extensive use. To address these issues, in this work, a scalable, simple, and eco-friendly graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation approach was elaborately designed to efficiently delaminate and disperse bulk g-C3N4 (B–CN), thereby obtaining low-cost and high-dispersion CN (G-CN). And the effect of B–CN, B–CN exfoliated without GQDs (U–CN) and G-CN on cement hydration was comprehensively investigated for the first time. Specifically, G-CN possessed a remarkably higher dispersion and a larger specific surface area (32.92 m2 g−1) increasing by 44.45% and 183.55% than U–CN and B–CN, respectively. And based on characteristics of G-CN and properties of GQDs, the exfoliation mechanism of g-C3N4 was ascribed to intercalation, exfoliation, and surfactant effects of GQDs. More importantly, highly dispersed G-CN was more propitious to accelerate cement hydration and refine microstructure, thereby dramatically boosting the compressive strength of cement paste at 7-day curing by 10.1% and 25.0% compared with that of U–CN and B–CN, respectively. This work not only provided a scalable, facile and environmentally-friendly approach to efficiently fabricate well dispersive g-C3N4, but also firstly testified their evident accelerating effect on cement hydration. These findings would be conducive to advancing the application of 2D nanomaterials in cement composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Exfoliation
Dispersity
Cement hydration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of GQDs, B–CN, G-CN and U–CN
Morphology and structure of GQDs and CN
Characterization of three types of CNs
Composition, microstructure and mechanics analyses of paste specimens
Effect of CN dispersion on cement hydration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation for B–CN, G-CN and U–CN.

Characterization of GQDs: (a, b) TEM images with inset depicting size distribution; (c) AFM image with inset showing the height profile along the green line; (d) XRD patterns; (e) Raman and (f) FT-IR spectra. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TEM images of (a) B–CN, (b) U–CN and (c) G-CN.
(a) XRD, (b) Raman and (c) FT-IR spectra of three classes of CNs.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) BJH pore-size distributions of three kinds of CNs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of all the CNs in different solutions: (a) aqueous solution; (b) saturated CH solution.
Schematic illustration of GQDs-assisted exfoliation mechanism for CN.
DTG curves at ages of (a) 7 d, (a) 14 d and (c) 28 d as well as (d) hydration degree of paste samples.
XRD patterns of various paste samples.
SEM images of 7-day curing paste samples: (a) blank group, (b) B–CN group, (c) U–CN group and G-CN group.
Pore-size distribution of various paste samples at ages of (a) 7 d and (b) 28 d.
(a) Compressive strength of different paste specimens, and (b) its correlation with hydration degree.
Pore texture parameters of all the CNs.

head.ce:title: Scalable preparation of high-dispersion g-C3N4 via GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation for accelerating cement hydration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, Guangdong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure
Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering
College of Civil and Transportation Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tremendous progress has been made in the employment of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials in cement composites. However, high cost and poor dispersion of 2D nanomaterials immensely hinder their extensive use. To address these issues, in this work, a scalable, simple, and eco-friendly graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation approach was elaborately designed to efficiently delaminate and disperse bulk g-C3N4 (B–CN), thereby obtaining low-cost and high-dispersion CN (G-CN). And the effect of B–CN, B–CN exfoliated without GQDs (U–CN) and G-CN on cement hydration was comprehensively investigated for the first time. Specifically, G-CN possessed a remarkably higher dispersion and a larger specific surface area (32.92 m2 g−1) increasing by 44.45% and 183.55% than U–CN and B–CN, respectively. And based on characteristics of G-CN and properties of GQDs, the exfoliation mechanism of g-C3N4 was ascribed to intercalation, exfoliation, and surfactant effects of GQDs. More importantly, highly dispersed G-CN was more propitious to accelerate cement hydration and refine microstructure, thereby dramatically boosting the compressive strength of cement paste at 7-day curing by 10.1% and 25.0% compared with that of U–CN and B–CN, respectively. This work not only provided a scalable, facile and environmentally-friendly approach to efficiently fabricate well dispersive g-C3N4, but also firstly testified their evident accelerating effect on cement hydration. These findings would be conducive to advancing the application of 2D nanomaterials in cement composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their multitudes of benefits such as easy processing, cheap cost, high efficiency and superior performance, cement composites serve as the most extensively employed material in the construction sector [1,2]. However, they still suffer from shortcomings of low compressive strength and poor impermeability with the rapid development of large-scale and high-rise engineering structures. Fortunately, nano-engineering is expected to provide a promising way to surmount these barriers, improving the properties of traditional cement-based materials by incorporating nanomaterials into a cement matrix or hybridizing nanomaterials onto cement surfaces. Up to now, a wild range of nanomaterials including SiO2 [3,4], TiO2 [5], Al2O3 [6], carbon nanotubes [7,8], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [9], graphene [10], graphene oxide [11,12], cellulose nanofiber [13,14] etc., have been demonstrated to accelerate cement hydration and refine micropore structure, thereby boosting the properties of mechanical strength and impermeability. Among them, benefiting from filling and bridging effects for cementitious materials, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials with the distinctive monolayer/multilayer sheet structure considerably enhance the hydration degree and prevent micro-crack expansion, thus the relevant research has been an attractive theme in recent years.

Despite tremendous progress, the research on 2D nanomaterials employment in cement composites is still inadequate. There are multitudes of significant issues yet to be addressed, among which the high cost and poor dispersion of 2D nanomaterials are primary challenges, immensely hindering their extensive use. Therefore, a great number of preparation protocols have been elaborately designed to obtain 2D nanomaterials containing graphene oxide and LDHs, but a few drawbacks such as complicated and time-consuming processes as well as expensive raw materials are still unavoidable, leading to the fact that it is difficult to achieve low-cost and scalable synthesis. For instance, Guo's group [15] and Lv's group [11] demonstrated significant reinforcing and toughening effects of graphene oxide (obtained by the common Hummer's method) for cement-based materials by multitudes of experiments. However, the Hummer's method including three complicated steps (oxidation, purification and exfoliation) spent more than 8 h, failing to meet the demands of time saving and simple operation. More seriously, the use of concentrated H2SO4 endangered the environment and threatened the operator safety. Recently, Khayat's group [16] prepared graphene oxide by an ultrasonic method to improve the chloride binding capacity of cement paste. The ultrasonic method avoided the use of strong acid, and simplified preparation processes, but it still took >2.5 h, and adopted expensive graphite oxide powder as the raw material, greatly increasing the cost of graphene oxide. Apart from graphene oxide, LDHs also cannot be obtained at low cost owing to the time-consuming preparation process. In detail, Guo's group [17] and Lu's group [18] revealed that LDHs could enhance the early strength and chloride binding performance of cement-based composites. Nevertheless, the whole preparation process of LDHs involving co-precipitation, suction filtration, washing and grinding was complex and time-consuming (more than 48 h), thereby augmenting their cost. Crucially, the starting materials of LDHs such as Fe(NO3)3, Al(NO3)3 and Ca(NO3)2 belonged to explosion-prone hazardous chemicals, making them difficult to obtain, and posing a threat to operator safety. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to develop low-cost 2D nanomaterials based on safe and available precursors to improve the performance of cement composites. The g-C3N4 (CN) as a graphite-like layered 2D nanomaterial supplies a promising alternative for cement-related applications due to their low toxicity, environmental friendliness as well as low-cost, one-step, large-scale and high-yield fabrication. Yang [19], Li [20], Ji [21] et al. prepared CN via one-step thermal polymerization employing melamine or urea as the raw material, and then incorporated CN into cement or onto cement surface to prepare photocatalytic cementitious composites for dye degradation and NOx removal. More importantly, CN serving as the nucleation site increased cement hydration degree, resulting in a densified microstructure and a large micro-hardness for cementitious composites [19]. However, in addition to this literature, there is no other investigation focusing on the CN effect on cement hydration. As a result, making more efforts to further explore the influence of CN on cement hydration is highly essential.

The economical synthesis of CN contributes to address the issue of high cost for 2D nanomaterials used in cement composites, but CN is still confronted with the challenge of poor dispersion as other 2D nanomaterials. Generally, CN produced by one-step thermal polymerization tends to be bulk, possessing a small surface area below 10 m2 g−1 [22]. As is well-known, on account of their high specific surface area and interface energy, nanomaterials inevitably agglomerate, and their agglomeration becomes deteriorating in cement matrix with strong alkali and high-metal ion content. Their poor dispersity not only greatly compromises their prospective effectiveness by causing stress concentration, exacerbating rheological behavior, etc., but also sharply increases the economic cost due to their compensatory addition to the cement matrix [4]. Consequently, to maximize their benefits, a wide variety of approaches have been developed to promote nanomaterials dispersity [4,5,13,23–25]. Similarly, various physical routes (e.g., ultrasonication exfoliation [26] and liquid ammonia-assisted lithiation [27,28]) and chemical treatment (e.g., acid/base stripping [29,30] and post-thermal oxidation etching [31]) have also been proposed to delaminate bulk CN into single/several layers. Nevertheless, few methods mentioned above are seen as scalable, efficient and environmentally-friendly, since several demerits are inescapable. For example, ultrasonication exfoliation have the low dispersion efficiency, and cannot eliminate nanomaterial reaggregation; While acid/base stripping and oxidation etching suffer from complicated process and environmental contamination due to use of toxic reagents. Therefore, it is urgently desired to achieve scalable and facile preparation of high-dispersion lamellar CN and comprehensively investigate its effect on cement hydration.

Recently, a novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation route was elaborately designed to efficiently produce well dispersive single-layered 2D graphene sheet from graphite powders [32,33], and the optimal exfoliation yield was up to 100% [32], providing a new strategy to prepare high-dispersion 2D nanomaterials. It should be emphasized that GQDs are a new class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, and normally defined as small carbon nanoparticles (<10 nm in size) with single/several-layer graphene in core and numerous functional groups at edge [34]. By means of their low-cost precursors, facile synthesis, favorable dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, GQDs have been extensively applied in biomedicine, optoelectronic devices, and functional materials [34]. In the exfoliation system above, GQDs function as not only intercalation/exfoliation agent to peel off graphene sheet, but also surfactant adsorbing to the exfoliated graphene sheet to stabilize it, thereby offering a more efficient exfoliation effect than that of GQDs-free ultrasonic exfoliation. Whereas, to the best of our knowledge, no research has been done to study the effect of lamellar CN dispersed by GQDs-assisted exfoliation on cement hydration.

Taking these considerations into account, this work elaborately designed a scalable, simple and eco-friendly GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation way to efficiently delaminate and disperse bulk CN (B–CN), and comprehensively investigated the effect of CN exfoliated with GQDs (G-CN) on cement hydration for the first time. In stark contrast with B–CN and CN exfoliated without GQDs (U–CN), G-CN possessed a remarkably higher dispersion demonstrated by morphology and structure characteristics. Based on characteristics of G-CN and properties of GQDs, the exfoliation mechanism of CN was rationally proposed. And by composition, microstructure and mechanics analyses of CN-reinforced paste specimens at different ages, the influence of B–CN, U–CN and G-CN on cement hydration and microstructure was systematically studied. These findings would contribute to address the issues of high cost and poor dispersity of 2D nanomaterials, thereby efficiently employing them in cement composites.

A grade PI 52.5R Portland cement (PC) conforming to the Chinese Standard GB175 and GB176 was used. Its physical properties and chemical compositions were listed in Table 1 and Table 2 , respectively. Urea, concentrated nitric acid, and naphthalene were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and employed without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was applied in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of GQDs, B–CN, G-CN and U–CN
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method based on the literature [33] with a marginal variation. 2.1 g naphthalene was suspended in 80 mL concentrated nitric acid and was agitated for 11 h on a magnetic stirrer at 80 °C. The obtained nitronaphthalene product was filtered and dried. 1 g nitronaphthalene was dissolved in 100 mL 0.1 M NaOH solution. After 8-h hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, purification (high-speed centrifugation and 0.2 μm membrane filtration), neutralization, freeze-drying, powdery GQDs was obtained.

B–CN i.e., bulk CN was formed by thermal polymerization method and improved on the basis of the literature [35]. 15 g of urea placed in a lidded quartz crucible was put into the muffle furnace, and then calcined at 550 °C for 2 h with a rate of 10 °C/min in air atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the yellowish B–CN was collected (Fig. 1 ). The resultant B–CN was ground into powder for further use. It should be pointed out that since the employed raw material (i.e., urea) was cheap [36], greatly reducing the cost of B–CN, which was beneficial to its large-scale application in cement-based materials.

G-CN i.e., high-dispersion CN was fabricated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation. In detail, 2.5 g B–CN was added into 500 mL GQDs solution with the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL (i.e., the mass ratio of B–CN and GQDs (B–CN/GQDs) was 50:1), followed by consecutive tip-sonication (720 W, 20 kHz) for 1 h in a water bath at 10 °C. The resultant solution was vacuum-dried to acquire solid-state G-CN for characterization (Fig. 1). It is worth noting that compared to the reported ultrasonic method [16], GQDs-assisted ultrasonic approach significantly reduced the process time to 1 h without increasing the cost and operation sequence.
U–CN shared the same preparation process with G-CN, only without GQDs adding (Fig. 1).

Three kinds of CNs (i.e., B–CN, U–CN, and G-CN) were employed to prepare cement paste specimens under same conditions, respectively. The preparation process was as follows. Firstly, B–CN/U–CN/G-CN was added into DI water, and dispersed by stirring and ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Secondly, the suspension was incorporated into cement with weight ratios of 0.005 for CN to cement and 0.4 for water to cement (w/c). After mixed by mechanical stirring, the paste was cast into 40 mm × 40 mm × 40 mm molds. Finally, these paste specimens were put into a curing chamber with temperature of 20±2 °C and relative humidity of >95%, demolded after 24 h and cured until related testing. The mix proportions were illustrated in Table 3 . It must be stressed that according to the prior investigation [37], 0.5–1.0 wt% dosage of CN tends to enhance cement hydration and improve mechanical characteristics of cement-based products. Hence, in this work, to evaluate the impact of CN dispersion on cement hydration, the 0.5 wt% dosage was chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of GQDs and CN
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Brucker MFP-3D infinity, Germany) were adopted to examine the morphologies of the produced GQDs and CN. The structure information of GQDs and CN was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brucker D8 Advance, Germany) patterns and Raman (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA), and Fourier infrared (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) spectra. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of CN was recorded using a micromertics Quantachrome NOVA4200E instrument (USA), and the pore size distribution was acquired by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory. The CN dispersion in saturated calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2, CH) was measured using ultraviolet–visible spectra (UV–Vis, PerkinElmer Lambda 750, USA).

According to Chinese national standard GB/T 17671-1999, compressive strength tests of specimens were carried out three times after 7-, 14- and 28-day curing. To explore the phase composition and hydration evolution, XRD patterns of 7-, 14- and 28-day curing samples were determined. Meantime, the thermogravimetric (TG) and first-order derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves of 7- and 28-day curing samples were recorded from 30 to 1000 °C in a flowing N2 atmosphere using a STA-409PC Thermogravimetric Analyzer (USA). Roughly 40 mg sample powder with sizes of less than 100 μm was used after hydration termination and grinding. Based on DTG and TG analyses, the hydration degree of different cement pastes was determined by equation (1)−(2) [24]:
(1)
W B
=
m
105 − 440 ° C
+
m
440 − 580 ° C
+ 0.41 ×
m
580 − 1000 ° C
(2)
α =
W B
0.24
× 100
where W
B and α represented chemically bound water content, and cement hydration degree, respectively; m105-440°C, m440-580°C and m580-1000°C were the weight losses derived from TG data in the regions of dehydration (105–440 °C), dehydroxylation (440–580 °C), and decarbonation (580–1000 °C), respectively; 0.41 was the conversion ratio of chemically bound water when converting CaCO3 to CH, and 0.24 represented the ratio of chemically bound water when cement completely hydrated.

The morphologies of 7-day age specimens were measured through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) analyses. BET studies and BJH theory were utilized to calculate the specific surface area and pore size distribution of 7- and 28-day curing specimens.

The typical TEM micrographs were presented in Fig. 2 a and b, suggesting that GQDs were torispherical and monodisperse with a size distribution from 1.5 to 4.5 nm and an average diameter of ca. 3.1 nm via randomly counting 100 particles. And GQDs possessed the crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in line with the graphene (100) plane. AFM image (Fig. 2c) revealed topographic heights of GQDs ranging from 0.7 to 1.3 nm, demonstrating that double- and three-layer GQDs were generated. XRD patterns (Fig. 2d) displayed an obvious broad peak at around 27.2° consistent with (002) plane of graphite, proving that GQDs had graphitic crystallinity structure but with some defects/disordered carbon. Raman spectrum supported the results of XRD. As shown in Fig. 2e, G peak at 1536 cm−1 for graphite carbon and D peak at 1369 cm−1 for disordered carbon were observed. FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2f) testified the existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups (−OH and C–O) on GQDs surface, which endowed GQDs with excellent water solubility. In addition, the presence of CC in benzene rings proved the graphite structure of GQDs, in accordance with the conclusions from TEM, XRD and Raman. To sum up, the as-prepared GQDs possessed a typic amphiphilic structure, composed of hydrophobic single/few-layer graphite structure in core and hydrophilic oxygenous groups at edge, thus endowing them with favorable exfoliation abilities [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of three types of CNs

ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of three kinds of CNs were directly observed by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, B–CN with a curled flake-like structure and a wrinkled edge tended to assemble, stack and densely aggregate owing to high-temperature polymerization, in good accordance with the morphologies of urea-derived CN [20,38]. After ultrasonic treatment, the aggregation of B–CN was mitigated, and the obtained U–CN with a length below 1 mm possessed a thinner curled lamellar structure and even included some transparent flakes (Fig. 3b), indicating that exfoliation effect rendered U–CN enlarger specific surface area and better dispersibility. In case of G-CN, it had an evident single/few-layer structure and a smaller length below 300 nm (Fig. 3c), revealing that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation process more efficiently delaminated B–CN into single/few-layer structures than pure ultrasonic treatment. Furthermore, GQDs incorporation under 720 W high-power ultrasound process was prone to alter the structure of CN, substantially reducing winkled edges and introducing abundant pores. Hence, the specific surface area and dispersion of G-CN were further enhanced， expected to accelerate cement hydration. It should be noted that on account of the low electro contrast between the GQDs and CN, it failed to find GQDs coupling on G-CN.

Exhaustive information about crystallographic structures of three classes of CNs was investigated by XRD and Raman (Fig. 4 a and b). As illustrated in XRD patterns (Fig. 4a), all the CNs obtained by different dispersion routes shared two characteristic peaks at 13° and 27.7°, related to in-plane packing ((100) plane) and interlayer stacking ((002) plane) of tetragonal CN [39]. Compared with the B–CN, the peak intensity and half-peak breadth of (002) plane in U–CN and G-CN were successively weakened and diminished, indicating the successful exfoliation and the introduction of lattice defects and induced by ultrasonic treatment, especially by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation [39,40], thereby conducing to enlarging specific surface area and dispersity of B–CN. This agreed well with observations of TEM images. Additionally, possibly owing to relatively low GQDs content, the structure of G-CN was negligibly changed after coupling with the GQDs, and there was no diffraction peak originating from GQDs. The inference mentioned above was verified by Raman spectra (Fig. 4b). All the CNs had a noticeable broad peak in the region of 1470–1518 cm−1, ascribed to the typical stretching vibration modes of CN and C–N heterocycles. Separately, the peak of G-CN and U–CN revealed downshift of 30 and 48 cm−1 in comparison with that of B–CN respectively, attributed to the phonon confinement effect caused by reduced layer numbers [41]. This firmly supported the fact that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation process could more efficiently delaminate B–CN into single/several layers. Furthermore, the peak at 2269 cm−1 of G-CN belonged to atmospheric stretching vibration of N potentially originating from the pore structure of G-CN, implying GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation created a great number of pores, which was in good consistence with the results drawn from TEM.

FT-IR measurement (Fig. 4c) was conducted to further probe the chemical structure of CNs. Apparently, all the CNs exhibited the typical absorption peaks at 810, 1000–1700 and 3000–3700 cm−1, assigned to the vibration mode of s-triazine units, stretching vibration of CN and stretching vibration of N–H/O–H, respectively [42,43]. This indicated that the exfoliated CN by ultrasonic process with/without GQDs kept the same chemical structure as their parent B–CN. Notably, presumably due to low GQDs addition, it was difficult to detect their absorption peaks from G-CN. Combing results of XRD, Raman and FT-IR analyses, it could be seen that ultrasonic treatment could convert B–CN to few-layer CN along with the introduction of lattice defects; while GQDs-assisted ultrasonic process were able to enhance the exfoliation efficiency and introduce multitudes of pores into the structure of B–CN, leading to the fact that G-CN possessed the largest specific surface area and highest dispersity. At the same time, regardless of ultrasonic treatment with or without GQDs, there was little influence on the chemical structure of CN.

To better understand the specific surface area and pore structure of three kinds of CNs，their N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and accompanying BJH pore-size distributions were studied. As described in Fig. 5 , all the adsorption-desorption curves complied with the type-IV adsorption characteristic with an H3-type hysteresis loop, unveiling that the numerous mesopores were located within the framework of CNs. With respect to that of B–CN and U–CN, the peak of G-CN in pore-size distribution markedly moved to the right, indicating that the interlayer spacing of G-CN became larger and had a favorable distribution [44], thereby endowing G-CN with better dispersion. The calculated BET specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume (VTotal) and pore size (DV) were listed in Table 4 . Remarkably, G-CN possessed a highest specific surface area of 32.92 m2 g−1, followed by U–CN (22.79 m2 g−1), increasing by 183.55% and 96.30% than that of B–CN, respectively. Simultaneously, the pore size of G-CN and U–CN had a downtrend, decreasing by 69.12% and 24.53% that that of B–CN, respectively, accredited to facts that GQDs effectively peeled off CN; In the meantime, GQDs were regarded to adsorb on the surface of G-CN [41]. Notably, in contrast to B–CN, total pore volume of G-CN dramatically increased by 69.32%, while that of B–CN almost remained constant, demonstrating that only GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could significantly introduce pores into the framework of CN, which was good agreement with conclusions of TEM and Raman.

According to Beer-Lambert law, the absorbance of solution is proportional to the solvent concentration, implying the higher the UV–vis absorbance value, the better the dispersion status of the solvent [7,45]. Therefore, UV–vis spectra of three types of CNs in different solutions were also adopted to evaluate their dispersibilities. As shown in Fig. 6 a, in the aqueous solution, G-CN possessed the highest absorbance value, and the second one was U–CN. Their absorbance values were much higher than B–CN, corroborating that ultrasonic exfoliation could effectively boost the dispersion of CN in aqueous solution, and the addition of GQDs achieved further enhancement. It is well acknowledged that strong alkali and high-metal ion content environment in the cement matrix makes nanomaterials easily agglomerate [45]. Hence, the UV–vis spectra of the whole CNs in saturated CH solution (acting as simulated pore solution) were investigated. As presented in Fig. 6b, passably on account of some agglomeration, the absorbance peaks of all the CNs shared an apparent change, but the absorbance values of G-CN and U–CN also ranked first and second respectively, meanwhile the absorbance value of U–CN went through a great decline. This meant that in the saturated CH solution, G-CN possessed a stronger capacity to resist agglomeration than B–CN, attributed to coupling of GQDs on its surface, which resulted in a more promising application in the cement matrix. In addition, owing of coupling of GQDs, G-CN exhibited caused a substantial red shift and a broader absorption shoulder, also found in other literatures [42,43].

The whole observations from TEM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR, BET and UV–vis spectra strongly convinced that compared with pure ultrasonic process, GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could more efficiently exfoliate CN, giving rise to more pore introduction, much larger specific surface area, smaller pore size and higher dispersity. More importantly, coupling GQDs conferred the CN with a more remarkable ability to effectively resist agglomeration in strong alkali and high-metal ion content conditions, thereby beneficial for their applications in cement composites. It is worth mentioning that as long as the mass ratio of B–CN and GQDs remains unchanged, the obtained G-CN possesses almost the same characteristics, thus the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation route provides a scalable preparation for lamellar CN.

In light of all the characteristics above, it could be found that a small amount of GQDs as exfoliation and surfactant agents greatly increased the dispersion of CN by peeling off and coupling. Their relevant exfoliation mechanism was proposed in Fig. 7 . By reason of its high specific surface area and interface energy, the pristine B–CN with a multilayered structure was in a state of agglomeration certified by TEM and XRD. While due to steric hindrance and electrostatic repulsion, GQDs with amphiphilic properties (hydrophobic single/few-layer graphite structure in core and hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups at edge) were monodisperse in aqueous and simulated pore solutions, evidenced by TEM and FT-IR. During the ultrasonic processing, the cavitation effect induced by ultrasonication provided the strong tensile stress [46] to weaken the van der Waals force among B-CNs, enlarging their interlayer space [32], thereby conferring the as-prepared U–CN with larger specific surface area and higher dispersity. Differently, after incorporation of GQDs, their hydrophobic graphite structure allowed them to adsorb onto B–CN surface through π-π, hydrophobic or van der Waals interactions [32], and their small size made them intercalate into the layered structure of B–CN and bind with layers [33]. Subsequently, serving as intercalation and exfoliation agent, GQDs strengthened the cavitation effect among layers of B–CN and offered a new strong electrostatic attraction force of H-bonds between GQDs oxygen-containing groups and water, thus more efficiently peeling off the single layer from B–CN, testified by TEM and BET. Furthermore, GQDs coupling on the surface of delaminated CN, functioned as the surfactant agent to effectively resist re-aggregation of CN, demonstrated by TEM, BET and UV–vis spectra. It's worth mentioning that as substantiated by TEM, Raman and BET, the high-power ultrasound of 720 W for the stripping technique in this work failed to leave the CN structure intact and easily introduced multitudes of pores. Nevertheless, these changes further enhanced the specific surface area and dispersity of exfoliated CN, obviously conducing to their application in cement-based materials. As aforementioned, the exfoliation mechanism of CN under the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic effect, was attributed to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition, microstructure and mechanics analyses of paste specimens
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that cement hydration degree has a significant effect on composition, microstructure and mechanics of paste specimens. As a result, the effect of three classes of CNs on paste specimens were comprehensively examined.

Proverbially, cement hydration products principally contain calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H), CH and ettringite/monosulfate (AFt/AFm). Whereas, some calcium carbonate (CaCO3) is inevitably generated due to reaction between CH and CO2 during the sample preparation for testing. The detailed composition information of different spate samples was collected by DTG and XRD measurement. Fig. 8 presented DTG diagrams of various paste specimens at 7-, 14- and 28-day curing. Normally, it is recognized that DTG mass loss humps at approximately 100 °C, 200 °C and 350 °C, the range of 450–580 °C and above 600 °C were ascribed to decomposition of C–S–H/AFt, AFm, CH and CaCO3, respectively [9]. Distinctly, all the specimens shared the similar DTG curves with four major peaks, but the intensities of these peaks varied, suggesting that all the CNs failed to alter hydration-product types, but had a great influence on their contents. Compared with plain group, all the CNs boosted the formation of hydration products involving C–S–H/AFt and AFm regardless of ages, and G-CN with highest dispersity possessed the most striking promotion effect for paste specimens, followed by U–CN. More specifically, based on equation (1)−(2) in Section 2.4, the hydration degree (α) of different paste specimens were quantitatively calculated (Fig. 8d). At 7-day age, α of B-CU, U–CN and G-CN groups rose by 0.05%, 1.49% and 3.03% respectively with respect to blank group, and α shared the similar trend but with a comparatively low increasing rate after 28-day age. Among them, regardless of ages, G-CN group possessed the largest α, and the second one was U–CN. All the observations from DTG demonstrated that all the CNs could accelerate the cement hydration, and the higher the dispersion of CN, the more noticeable the acceleration of cement hydration (particularly at the early-age stage), assigned to the fact that high-dispersion CN supplied more nucleation sites for hydration products.

The results from DTG were well-supported by the evidence from XRD patterns. As displayed in Fig. 9 , all the XRD profiles revealed consistent peaks attributed to the hydration product of CH (peaks at 18.1o, 28.7o, 34o, 47.1o, 50.8o and 54.3o), unhydrated products of tricalcium silicate (C3S, peaks at 32.6o) and dicalcium silicate (C2S, peaks at 32o and 41.2o) as well as the carbonization product of CaCO3 (peaks at 29.4o) [3,9], meaning that the introduction of CN could not form new hydration products. In general, the intensity of main diffraction peak at 18.1o from CH is directly proportional to the quantity of the hydration production, thereby adopted to assess the hydration degree [9,47]. Obviously, the peak at 18.1o of CH for all the groups greatly increased from 7- to 28-day curing. At the same time, regardless of ages, CN-containing groups had more intense peaks of CH by comparison with control group, and G-CN with the largest specific surface area and the best dispersibility possessed the maximum CH peak. Therefore, CNs could promote the generation of hydration products by seeding effects to provide nucleation sites, and the hydration process was significantly increased by high-dispersion G-CN, well coinciding with the results of TG.

SEM and BJH were employed to determine the effect of CN on the microstructure of paste samples. As shown in SEM, as for blank group at 7-day curing, there were plenty of unhydrated cement particles conglutinated by a few hydration products of fibrous and granular C–S–H, fine needle-like AFt, and flaky CH, making the internal structure loose and porous (Fig. 10 a). With the addition of B–CN, unhydrated cement particles efficaciously reduced and the hydration products overtly increased, improving integration of internal structure. But resulting from their poor dispersity, the degree of cement hydration was still insufficient, thus some large pores were ineluctable (Fig. 10b). As the dispersity of CN was risen, the internal structure of paste was further refined with the noticeable growth of hydration products. In detail, thick rod-like Aft was generated, and bushy C–S–H gel increased. More importantly, abundant hydration products tended to interlock, interweave and uniformly distribute, leading to the compact and optimized internal structure (Fig. 10c and d), which were considered to enhance the mechanical performance of hardened pastes. In addition, compare with U–CN group (Fig. 10c), G-CN reinforced paste produced more numerous aggregated and densely packed C–S–H gels almost covering crevices and pores, thus significantly reducing microfractures (Fig. 10d). These findings above implied that G-CN prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation could more uniformly disperse in the cement matrix. The better dispersed G-CN was able to fill up the pores in cement paste to optimize the pore size distribution, thereby enhancing the compactness of the hardened cement paste. At the same time, it has been proved that high-dispersion nanomaterials could provide more nucleation sites for the nucleation, precipitation and growth of hydration products [48,49], thus greatly accelerating cement hydration and improving the micro and macro properties of cement-based materials. Consequently, well-dispersed G-CN with the higher specific surface area resulted in a significant increase in the available nucleation sites for hydration products, which made the nucleation effect more pronounced than that of B–CN and U–CN.

The pore structure of different paste specimens was further examined by BJH method. As displayed in Fig. 11 a, the pore size distribution was markedly changed and the average pore size was lowered after adding CN at 7-day age, attributable to nucleation and filling effects of CN [19]. Specifically, for all the samples, G-CN endowed the paste with the most gel pores between 5 and 10 nm and the smallest pore volume. And central peaks of blank, B–CN, U–CN and G-CN groups were 34.1, 33.2, 32.5, and 31.3 nm respectively. This left shift of central peaks demonstrated that CNs in the cement matrix promoted cement hydration and densified pore microstructure, and their increasing dispersion gave rise to further optimization of the effect mentioned, precisely according to the conclusions of DTG and SEM. With the curing prolonged to 28 d (Fig. 11b), the influence of CN on pore-size distribution declined. For example, the primary peaks of blank, B–CN, U–CN and G-CN samples were 31.2, 31.1, 30.6, and 30.1 nm, respectively, and G-CN still possessed most gel pores and the smallest primary peak. This indicated that the positive effect of CNs on cement hydration degree after 28-day age became modest, but improved dispersion of CN was still beneficial to optimize the pore structure of pastes, in accordance with the observation of DTG.

All the composition and microstructure analyses mentioned above substantially corroborated that via nucleation and filling behaviors, CNs, particularly highly dispersed G-CN, could accelerate the cement hydration to form more hydration products, and optimize the pore-size distribution to refine microstructure of cement matrix. This was expected to facilitate the strength development of cement composites, since mechanical performance of hardened pastes was primarily dependent on characteristics of hydration products and microstructures. Hence, the compressive strength of various paste specimens was investigated.

As depicted in Fig. 12 a, regardless of ages, three kinds of CNs had a positive influence on compressive strength compared to the blank group. To be specific, for 7-day curing pastes, the compressive strength of B–CN, U–CN, and G-CN groups raised by 11.9%, 13.5% and 25.0% respectively compared with the plain group. Among them, high-dispersion G-CN possessed the largest compressive strength of 56.5 MPa. In case of 14- and 28-day age pastes, the trend of CNs influence on compressive strength was similar with that of 7-day curing pastes, but the reinforcing effect of CNs gradually diminished. For instance, with respect to that of reference group, the compressive strength of G-CN group promoted by 15.8% and 5.0% at ages of 14 d and 28 d, respectively. Furthermore, as presented in Fig. 12b, there was a strong linear correlation between cement hydration degree and compressive strength of cement paste, and the compressive strength enhanced with hydration increasing, agreeing with the previous research [13]. The conclusions above confirmed that CNs could accelerate the strength development of cement pastes, and the improvement of CNs dispersion was conducive to further heightening the effect, ascribed to the enhancement effect of CN on cement hydration and refinement of microstructure, which was certified by DTG, XRD, SEM and BJH. In addition, it was believed that the compressive strength of G-CN reinforced paste specimens could be further increased by adding more GQDs to disperse CN or tuning the G-CN dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CN dispersion on cement hydration
ce:section.ce:para: According to the characterization (TEM, XRD, Raman, BET and UV–vis spectra) of CNs, G-CN prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation possessed the largest specific surface area and the highest dispersity, followed by U–CN fabricated by pure ultrasonic treatment. Based on the findings from composition, microstructure and mechanics analyses of paste specimens, it was observed that all the CNs played a positive role in the cement hydration and microstructure (particularly at the early-age stage), and the dispersion of CN mainly determined the degree of positive impact: the higher the dispersion of CN, the higher the hydration degree of cement. As a result, G-CN endowed the paste with more hydration products, more refined microstructure and larger compressive strength.

In summary, this work firstly provided a scalable, simple, and eco-friendly GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation way to efficiently delaminate and disperse CN for accelerating cement hydration. The primary conclusions were drawn as follows:
(1)
Under ultrasonic treatment, amphiphilic GQDs tuned the structure of CN, substantially reducing winkled edges and introducing abundant pores, thus G-CN possessed a remarkably higher dispersion and a larger specific surface area of 32.92 m2 g−1 increasing by 183.55% and 44.45% respectively in stark contrast with B–CN and U–CN. More importantly, the obtained G-CN possessed almost the same characteristics when the mass ratio of B–CN and GQDs remained unchanged. Hence, the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation route provided a scalable preparation for high-dispersion CN.
(2)
In light of G-CN characteristics and GQDs properties, the exfoliation mechanism of CN by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was assigned to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs.
(3)
Based on composition, microstructure, and mechanics analyses of diverse paste specimens, it could be found that the CN dispersion caused a remarkable effect on cement hydration and microstructure, particularly at the early-age stage. High-dispersion G-CN was more propitious to accelerate cement hydration and refine microstructure, thereby dramatically boosting the compressive strength of cement paste at 7-day curing by 10.1% and 25.0% compared with that of U–CN and B–CN, respectively. It was ascribed to more efficient nucleation and filling effects offered by well dispersive G-CN.

This work not only provided a scalable, facile and environmentally-friendly approach to efficiently fabricate well dispersive CN, but also testified their evident accelerating effect on cement hydration. These findings are expected to address the issues of high cost and poor dispersity of 2D nanomaterials, thereby efficiently employing them in cement composites.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China, NSFC-Shandong Joint Fund (No. U2006223), the Science and Technology Project of Shenzhen, China (No. JCYJ20190808151011502), the Key-Area Research and Devel-opment Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2019B111107003), the Guangdong University Student Science and Technology Innovation Cultivation Special Fund Project (No. pdjh2022a0434). Furthermore, the authors would also like to thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for TEM and XPS measurements. In addition, the authors thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University for the assistance with analysis.
